item.title: Committee flags K4.5 billion Connect PNG anomaly
item.description: THE Public Accounts Committee has discovered that about K4.5 billion had been spent under the Connect PNG programme since 2020 without the establishment of its legislated development trust fund. Committee chairman and Talasea MP Freddie Kumai tabled the committee’s report of its inquiry into the Government’s K20 billion infrastructure development programme scheduled to be completed by 2040.
item.content:encoded:
THE Public Accounts Committee has discovered that about K4.5 billion had been spent under the Connect PNG programme since 2020 without the establishment of its legislated development trust fund.

Committee chairman and Talasea MP Freddie Kumai tabled the committee’s report of its inquiry into the Government’s K20 billion infrastructure development programme scheduled to be completed by 2040.

He said that the inquiry last month highlighted funding and capacity issues with the implementation of the programme, including non-compliance with the International Federation of Consulting Engineers standards.

“Among the findings, the committee noted failures in the establishment and oversight of the trust account,” Kumai said.

“There were substantial deficiencies in the establishment and administration of the trust fund account, including weaknesses in governance and administrative processes, and a failure by the Connect PNG board to exercise adequate oversight over the funding process.

“The committee observed failures by contractors and engineers to comply with FIDIC contractual standards and requirements, which are essential for proper project management, contract administration, and accountability in infrastructure delivery.

“The report identifies key challenges, highlights areas of inefficiency and non-compliance, and sets out clear and practical recommendations to strengthen governance, improve financial management, and ensure that the Connect PNG programme achieves its intended objectives.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: International pressure must not dictate law enforcement: MP
item.description: POLICE Minister Sir John Pundari says international pressure should not determine how the country detects, investigates and prosecutes crimes in Papua New Guinea. He said the country should follow the normal practice of law enforcement in PNG, rather than complying with a list sent from abroad.
item.content:encoded:
POLICE Minister Sir John Pundari says international pressure should not determine how the country detects, investigates and prosecutes crimes in Papua New Guinea.

He said the country should follow the normal practice of law enforcement in PNG, rather than complying with a list sent from abroad.

“While the Financial Action Task Force evaluation process is a journey to ensure our systems meet contemporary standards, our actions should not be solely in response to an international list,” Sir John said.

“We must act for the good of our country.

“To address the years of inaction, the government has taken decisive steps to bolster our investigative capacity.

“As a result, two Commonwealth officers are now embedded within the National Fraud and Anti-Corruption Directorate to provide elite-level support and mentorship.

“The Government has also ordered the establishment of the Unexplained Wealth Investigation Unit under the Proceeds of Crimes Act. That means if someone has riches they cannot explain, then this unit will find out why.

“Let me be clear that no one should stand in the way of these key interventions.

“These are the tools that will not only assist in removing PNG from the grey-list but are necessary intervention not only to restore the integrity of our financial system but to save our country from corrupt and ill gains, money laundering, corrupt money and commission payments changing hands in places like Australia, Singapore, the Philippines and within our country,” he said.

Sir John was responding to a comment by Bulolo MP Sam Basil Jr that Papua New Guineans were falling victims to corruption and fraud involving public servants.

The matter raised was on public facilities and land that were illegally transferred or stolen from the public. 

The specific matter referenced is currently before the courts.

It relates to the land and assets of the Highlands Farmers and Settlers Association in Banz, Goroka and Dumpu.

Basil asked the Police and Lands departments what action was being taken to address such issues given that the country was greylisted.

Basil said: “The Public Accounts Committee during Sir John Hickey’s days branded that the most corrupt departments were the Lands and the National Housing Corporation.

“The inaction by police over fraud and corruption is getting PNG on the grey-list and the corruption related to land and assets will not attract investment.

“Is the Government giving attention to the National Fraud Squad and giving them resources as this 10-year complaint and court decision is taking too long?” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Marape plans to outlaw polygamy to protect women
item.description: PRIME Minister James Marape says he intends to push laws that will outlaw polygamy in Papua New Guinea. Polygamy is the practice of men having more than one wife.
item.content:encoded:
PRIME Minister James Marape says he intends to push laws that will outlaw polygamy in Papua New Guinea.

Polygamy is the practice of men having more than one wife.

Marape described the move as part of a broader national effort to protect women, strengthen families, and restore respect for women in the country.

Speaking during discussions on gender-based violence and women’s issues on Wednesday, Marape said the Government was considering a proposal that would see new laws introduced to end polygamy.

He said while Papua New Guinea’s rich cultural traditions must be respected, certain practices that undermine the dignity and security of women must be addressed through national reform.

“Some good aspects of our culture must be preserved, but other practices that harm our families must be left behind,” Marape said.

“Polygamy is one such issue that has contributed to many broken homes, children being left without proper support, and women being treated unfairly.”

He plans to present a policy paper to the National Executive Council to begin the process of introducing legislation that would outlaw polygamy, with the proposed law expected to take effect around 2030 onward.

Under the proposed approach, existing arrangements would not be automatically invalidated, but the law would prohibit new polygamous arrangements in the future.

“This is a reform that we must consider carefully, but it is necessary if we are to protect the mothers and daughters of our country,” he said.

Marape said the proposal reflected the Government’s commitment to addressing deeper social causes of gender-based violence and inequality.

He said: “A woman must no longer be seen as an asset or something that can be acquired from time to time,” Marape said.

“Women are equal partners in our families and our communities.

“God created them equal, and they must be treated with dignity and respect.

 “Men who walk away from their responsibilities, who leave behind women and children, will suffer the consequences.

“Our laws must ensure that women and children are protected and that family responsibilities are respected.

“That is the kind of country we must build for the next generation of Papua New Guineans,” he said.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Bill tabled to protect rights of customary landowners
item.description: THE Special Parliamentary Committee on Customary Land and Land Reforms has proposed a bill to protect the rights of customary landowners to contribute to sustainable development. Committee chairman and Hiri-Koiari MP Keith Iduhu said the bill was one of the recommendations by the committee to improve land governance in the country, based on regional consultations and other discussions.
item.content:encoded:
THE Special Parliamentary Committee on Customary Land and Land Reforms has proposed a bill to protect the rights of customary landowners to contribute to sustainable development.

Committee chairman and Hiri-Koiari MP Keith Iduhu said the bill was one of the recommendations by the committee to improve land governance in the country, based on regional consultations and other discussions.

Iduhu, who tabled the report in Parliament yesterday, said the legislation would also allow for the better management and protection of customary land.

“Land in Papua New Guinea is not simply property. It is identity, spiritual connection, food security, and social stability,” he said.

“It is protected under our Constitution through the recognition of custom as law.

“Nearly ninety-seven percent of our land remains under customary tenure, making our country one of the strongest customary landholding societies in the world.

“For this reason, the committee has not only conducted an inquiry but also prepared a legislative proposal – the draft Customary Land Tenure Bill – to operationalise the reform framework outlined in this report.”

Iduhu said the Bill aimed to strengthen:

Iduhu said: “Our land is our life. The strength of our customary land system lies in its cultural legitimacy.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG’s Alex Johnston breaks NRL try-scoring record
item.description: Papua New Guinean Kumul and South Sydney Rabbitohs winger Alex Johnston has etched his name into National Rugby League history, becoming the competition’s greatest-ever try scorer. Johnston crossed for two tries in the first half of South Sydney’s clash against the Sydney Roosters tonight, scoring his 212th and 213th career tries to equal and then surpass the long-standing record.
item.content:encoded:
Papua New Guinean Kumul and South Sydney Rabbitohs winger Alex Johnston has etched his name into National Rugby League history, becoming the competition’s greatest-ever try scorer.

Johnston crossed for two tries in the first half of South Sydney’s clash against the Sydney Roosters tonight, scoring his 212th and 213th career tries to equal and then surpass the long-standing record.

The milestone cements his place in NRL folklore as one of the game’s greatest finishers.

Allianz Stadium erupted as Rabbitohs supporters flooded the venue with cheers, forcing a pause in play for more than 10 minutes as fans celebrated the historic moment.

Among those witnessing the occasion were former Lae Snax Tigers premiership-winning captain Shalom Bire, Lae Biscuit Company Group Chairman Ian Chow, and General Manager John Francis Chow.

Chow described the moment as “electrifying”.

“(There was) huge crowd support, with fans screaming when AJ scored the try to break the 53-year try-scoring record,” he said.

He said it was an “emotional and exciting feeling that one of our own, and an ambassador of our Lae Biscuit product (Pawa), stood tall tonight. It is a historic moment for the NRL and Papua New Guinea.”

Johnston, 31, will now be immortalised in NRL history as the greatest try scorer the game has seen.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: History beckons for Johnston
item.description: AS traditional rivalry between South Sydney Rabbitohs and Sydney Roosters takes centre stage in tonight’s NRL Round 2 match, so is Papua New Guinea’s own Kumul winger, Alex Johnston, who is set to go down in history as the greatest try-scorer of all time. Johnston, 31, is two tries away of becoming the NRL’s greatest ever try-scorer — a record that was set by Ken Irvine and has not been broken in
item.content:encoded:
AS traditional rivalry between South Sydney Rabbitohs and Sydney Roosters takes centre stage in tonight’s NRL Round 2 match, so is Papua New Guinea’s own Kumul winger, Alex Johnston, who is set to go down in history as the greatest try-scorer of all time.

Johnston, 31, is two tries away of becoming the NRL’s greatest ever try-scorer — a record that was set by Ken Irvine and has not been broken in the last 57 years, and in more than 12,000 NRL matches. The record has stood the test of modern rugby league since 1998.

Irvine was best known as the fastest winger of his era and the all-time leading try-scorer in the premiership with 212 tries, playing for North Sydney and Manly-Warringah.

The Allianz Stadium was sold out since Monday as the NRL board warned of fines against fans who choose to run onto the field when Johnston breaks the record.   

While the outside noise and pressure is mounting for Johnston to break the record, the Rabbitohs winger remains calm and is focused on helping his side get their second win this season.

“There’s been a flood of messages from families, friends and also other NRL players all wanting to see me break that record,” Johnston told The National. 

“A lot of tries just come naturally as I play and try to do my job. It helps when I’ve had a lot of top class players feeding me the ball over the years, like Trell (Latrell Mitchell) and Cody (Cody Walker).

“The main thing I work on is getting my timing right at the end of the play so it puts me in the best position to score tries,” Johnston said.

“I just want to live my dream and play (rugby) league. I never thought I’d be in this position, and no one thought this record was even possible to break. So to be this close is mind blowing,” he added.

Johnston won the premiership with the Rabbitohs in his rookie year (2014), when he topped the season with 21 tries in just 18 matches.

In 2015, he went on to represent New South Wales as 18th men in State of Origin Game 3 and later played for the Indigenous All Stars.

Since his debut in 2014, Johnston has averaged 18 tries or more in the last 13 seasons.  One of his greatest fans, close family friend and Snax Tigers franchise manager Ian Chow praised the Kumuls/Bunnies’ winger for his effort.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Inaugural recruits to enjoy a once-in-a-lifetime experience
item.description: INAUGURAL recruits of the PNG Chiefs moving to Port Moresby will enjoy a once-in-a-lifetime experience when the team enters the National Rugby League (NRL) competition in 2028, says franchise general manager Michael Chammas. Speaking in response to security concerns raised on Sports Entertainment Network (SEN) League Radio, Chammas said part of his role is to help change the narrative about Papua
item.content:encoded:
INAUGURAL recruits of the PNG Chiefs moving to Port Moresby will enjoy a once-in-a-lifetime experience when the team enters the National Rugby League (NRL) competition in 2028, says franchise general manager Michael Chammas.

Speaking in response to security concerns raised on Sports Entertainment Network (SEN) League Radio, Chammas said part of his role is to help change the narrative about Papua New Guinea and its capital Port Moresby.

“A lot of the things you hear or Google about Papua New Guinea and Port Moresby are not the full picture,” he said. “I’ve had these conversations with agents, players and even with my own family. If you rely only on Google, you might think the worst straight away, but that’s not the reality.”

Chammas acknowledged that security is an important priority, but said strong systems will be in place to ensure players and their families are well protected.

“Of course we have to take precautions and make sure security is paramount,” he said. “But these players are going to be absolute heroes over there. Safety won’t be their issue.”

He explained that the club is planning a well-structured lifestyle for players, including accommodation in a secure village environment, organised transport and strong support systems for families.

Chammas stressed that the project is being supported by the club, the NRL and government partners to ensure the best possible environment for players relocating to PNG.

He said the goal now is to shift the perception about the country and show players what life in PNG can really offer.

“My job right now when I talk to players is to change the narrative around Papua New Guinea, because perception is not reality,” he said. Chammas admitted that like any nation, PNG has challenges, but he believes the positive experiences players will have will far outweigh those concerns.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: MoU secures safety plan for 8th PNG Games
item.description: THE 8th PNG Games will be staged in Mendi, Southern Highlands, in either June or July. A memorandum of understanding (MoU) on security arrangements was signed yesterday between the PNG Sports Foundation (PNGSF) and the Southern Highlands Unity Games coordination unit.
item.content:encoded:
THE 8th PNG Games will be staged in Mendi, Southern Highlands, in either June or July.

A memorandum of understanding (MoU) on security arrangements was signed yesterday between the PNG Sports Foundation (PNGSF) and the Southern Highlands Unity Games coordination unit.

Department of Prime Minister and National Executive Council (PMNEC) safety and security advisor Jerry Geno will oversee the delivery of safety and security measures for the event.

“We meet here to sign an MoU that’s not legally binding. However, it allows PNGSF to provide support and for the safety and security committee to ensure a safe venue for the Unity Games in Mendi,” Geno said.

“This understanding will fast-track some of the things we want to do but have been delaying.”

Deputy Police Commissioner (special operations) Donald Yamasombi described the occasion as significant, stressing that safety and security are critical to the success of any major event.

“We have already prepared our concept of operations for the Unity Games, but we are yet to set a date to launch it,” he said.

“Time is against us, but this partnership with PNGSF ensures accountability in how funds are managed. Though the MoU is not legally binding, it guides processes for assessing and processing claims, ensuring transparency in expenditure.” Yamasombi added that procurement, allowances, and other costs will be closely monitored, while St John’s Ambulance and the Fire Service will establish facilities in Mendi during the Games.

PNGSF chief executive officer Micah James thanked the office of the chief secretary, the coordination team, and the safety and security committee for mobilising resources towards a common cause.

“One of the important areas where youths can come together to identify, meet, and empower themselves moving forward is through sports,” James said.

“This event gives them the opportunity to recognise their role in nation-building.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Thank you to Papua New Guinea Angels
item.description: The Papua New Guinea Angels’ mission to collaborate with local doctors at Port Moresby General Hospital is a beacon of hope for many critically-ill patients. It’s heartwarming to see medical professionals coming together to make a difference in their communities.
item.content:encoded:
The Papua New Guinea Angels’ mission to collaborate with local doctors at Port Moresby General Hospital is a beacon of hope for many critically-ill patients.

It’s heartwarming to see medical professionals coming together to make a difference in their communities.

Dr Marc Coughlan and the team are true champions and their dedication to improving healthcare in PNG is truly commendable.

The fact that they’re not only providing surgical operations but also working alongside local doctors is fantastic.

This collaboration will undoubtedly enhance the skills and knowledge of our local medical professionals, empowering them to tackle complex cases in the future.

It’s a win-win situation — the patients receive top-notch treatment, and our doctors get valuable experience and training.

Kudos to the PNG Angels for choosing Port Moresby General Hospital as their base for this mission.

The hospital serves a vast population, and this partnership will undoubtedly have a significant impact on the lives of many families.

The team’s tireless efforts will bring smiles to countless faces, and that’s something to cherish.

It’s heartening to see organisations like PNG Angels taking proactive steps to address the healthcare challenges we face.

Their selfless work is a testament to the power of compassion and kindness.

May their efforts inspire others to follow suit and contribute to making PNG a healthier, happier place for all.

The work of PNG Angels also highlights the importance of healthcare partnerships and collaborations.

By working together, we can achieve far more than we could alone.

This mission is a shining example of what’s possible when we put the needs of others first.

I’m also excited to see the impact this will have on the local community.

Not only will patients receive life-changing treatment, but the local doctors and medical staff will also benefit from the knowledge and expertise shared by the PNG Angels team. 

Hope the PNG Angels’ mission brings healing and hope to many more patients in the days ahead.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Address lawlessness at Gereka Market
item.description: GEREKA Market situated just near Gereka Police Post becomes an eyesore daily with a particular ethnic group drinking and behaving disorderly and playing boom box at a high volume and not showing any respect for the public and residents. Gereka is a newly-emerged suburb where people built new homes to enjoy privacy, but are now facing the same negativities they ran away from at other congested and
item.content:encoded:
GEREKA Market situated just near Gereka Police Post becomes an eyesore daily with a particular ethnic group drinking and behaving disorderly and playing boom box at a high volume and not showing any respect for the public and residents.

Gereka is a newly-emerged suburb where people built new homes to enjoy privacy, but are now facing the same negativities they ran away from at other congested and crime-riddled settlements in the city.

For a safer and impressive image of every new suburb or community within the periphery of NCD, the two elected representatives — NCD Governor Powers Parkop and Port Moresby South MP Justin Tkatchenko — must take heed to this call and collaborate with police and address the the issues at Gereka Market, or even shut it down.

All auxiliary police personnel at Gereka are also well aware of this eyesore and lawlessness happening right before their eyes on a daily basis.

When people have no respect for the native Papuans, Koiaris and the Goilalas, the law must be enforced and stop the disorder going on in Gereka and other parts of Port Moresby. 

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Fix inequalities first before electing PM
item.description: I STRONGLY oppose the idea of allowing the nation to vote directly for the Prime Minister. Our current Westminster system must first be made to work effectively before any discussion of adopting a more complicated presidential system.
item.content:encoded:
I STRONGLY oppose the idea of allowing the nation to vote directly for the Prime Minister.

Our current Westminster system must first be made to work effectively before any discussion of adopting a more complicated presidential system.

There are growing concerns that a few ethnic groups are attempting to dominate the prime minister’s position and political landscape due to their increasing population and political influence.

If this continues unchecked, it risks concentrating political power and control of national resources in the hands of a few, instead of ensuring there is fair distribution for all Papua New Guineans.

Programmes such as Connect PNG also raise serious questions.

While roads are important, why is there comparatively little investment in provincial roads, local level government roads and essential maritime infrastructure such as wharfs and jetties?

Many coastal and island provinces rely heavily on sea transport, yet funding for this sector appears limited.

How many wharfs and jetties have actually been built under Connect PNG?

Such imbalances risk contradicting the spirit of the Preamble of the Constitution, which calls for fair and equitable development for all citizens.

Our founding father, Grand Chief Sir Michael Somare, often reminded leaders to remember those who are disadvantaged and ensure development reaches all regions.

Sadly, many leaders today seem unable to follow his example of fairness and compassion.

If the current system cannot deliver equal development and responsible leadership, then serious discussions about stronger regional autonomy and governance reforms may become necessary to protect the interests of all provinces and citizens.

PNG must remain united, but unity must also come with fairness, balance and responsible leadership.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: US stint an eye-opener for Samania
item.description: WHEN Samania Ned was nominated to attend an international visitor leadership programme in the United States, she saw it as an opportunity to learn more about cyber-security. And she not only learned more, but it also changed her views on leadership and responsibility.
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Samania Ned was nominated to attend an international visitor leadership programme in the United States, she saw it as an opportunity to learn more about cyber-security.

And she not only learned more, but it also changed her views on leadership and responsibility.

“I come from a technical background where systems and processes are everything. What I saw in the US was more of a focus on designing technology around people – about trust, behavior and inclusion, and not just infrastructure.”

Samania, an information and communications technology professional based in Port Moresby, attended the programme on cyber-security and public-private partnerships, sponsored by the US State Department.

“I was approached by the US embassy in Port Moresby. It’s a programme where participants are nominated based on their leadership and work in their field.”

She was recognised through professional networks supporting women leaders in technology, a sector she described as small but highly influential.

The programme brought together people from more than 20 countries. It included visits to Washington DC., Cleveland and Atlanta.

It is the State Department’s premier professional exchange programme which builds long-term relationships between leaders from around the world and their US counterparts through short-term visits focused on professional collaboration, mutual understanding and people-to-people ties.

We talked about culture, religion and politics in our own regions. It gave me a global perspective and helped me understand how leadership in cyber-security is evolving worldwide.”

The itinerary was designed to explore cyber-security through collaboration between the Government, private sector and academia.

She met with university researchers, legal experts, technology executives and policy specialists, and learned a lot from them during informal discussions.

“We also talked about culture, religion and politics in our own regions. It gave me a global perspective and helped me understand how leadership in cyber-security is evolving worldwide.”

One of her takeaways was how she has changed from purely technical thinking to a more people-centred approach. It influenced her personal and professional life.

“Personally, it made me more self-aware. When you’re moving into leadership, you have to think about how your decisions affect others.”

Samania is the operations manager at the Digital Identification Bureau, also known as YuTru. She manages the technical aspects of the project, with a focus on system security and architecture.

Since returning from the US, her work has increasingly focused on policy alignment and long-term sustainability, plus technical delivery.

“Instead of focusing only on technology, we are looking at how systems, regulations and people work together. It helps us collaborate with government partners as Papua New Guinea moves forward with digital transformation.”

Samania also launched coding initiatives for children in Port Moresby, encouraging students to think creatively and explore opportunities in technology.

She was recently the guest speaker and facilitator at the Reverend Sione Kami church back-to-school boot-camp. She taught children how to build a website, guiding them step-by-step through basic design and functionality.

“For many of them, it was the first time that they realised they could create something on the internet themselves. That kind of confidence can change how young people see their future.”

She has also been involved in mentoring and public speaking sessions in schools and community events. Earlier this year, she addressed postgraduate students during the orientation at the University of Technology – where she had earned a master’s degree – sharing how education and global exposure could open “doors” for them.

The US programme experience has also secured her an international network of peers. She remains in contact with fellow participants from around the world, including peers from Tonga and Fiji, exchanging ideas with them.

“If someone needs insight into how a system works in another country, we reach out. Those relationships have continued long after the programme ends.”

She encourages professionals in ICT, health and other sectors to attend the programme if nominated.

“It’s only a few weeks, but it’s enough time to change how you see leadership and your role. What matters is coming back and applying what you learned.”

Her message to young people and early-career professionals is simple: “Never stop learning. Every opportunity is an opportunity to grow.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: PNG Chiefs: How rugby league can foster a genuine fair go
item.description: Papua New Guinea’s new national rugby league team, the PNG Chiefs, has a big bill to fill, with Prime Minister James Marape describing the venture as a “national unification strategy”. Yet with national pride not in short supply in PNG, it is the more nuanced objectives of the project that could prove most beneficial.
item.content:encoded:
Papua New Guinea’s new national rugby league team, the PNG Chiefs, has a big bill to fill, with Prime Minister James Marape describing the venture as a “national unification strategy”.

Yet with national pride not in short supply in PNG, it is the more nuanced objectives of the project that could prove most beneficial.

High on the list should be collective social mobility.

Although PNG is one of the most multicultural and linguistically diverse countries in the world, its vast geography and limited infrastructure mean many communities remain physically and socially disconnected, leaving few experiences that are truly shared.

Broadcast coverage has allowed the game to penetrate even the most remote areas, creating shared national moments during events like NRL grand finals and State of Origin matches.

The prime minister has compared this reach to the unifying power of the 1995 Springbok Rugby World Cup win.

Like rugby union in South Africa, rugby league in PNG carries emotional weight far beyond the field.

It can also reflect the country’s deeper tensions, with calls in the past to suspend live broadcasts because of the violence that sometimes follows.

As with any diverse society, the concept of unity relies on the belief that the system is fair for everyone; that where you are born does not determine where you end up.

Australia, considered one of the world’s most successful multicultural societies, understands this through the idea of the “fair go”.

But even here, that ideal is under pressure.

 In PNG, where inequality is far more acute and public services are failing, that strain is immense.

Early reports of tax concessions for NRL players and accommodation in exclusive Port Moresby hotels have already highlighted the stark gap between elite and expatriate living standards and those of PNG’s working class, who reside in settlements without reliable water or power.

As a result, the “unity” project has the potential to reinforce perceptions of entrenched economic division rather than social cohesion.

As with any diverse society, the concept of unity relies on the belief that the system is fair for everyone; that where you are born does not determine where you end up.

Beyond the professional franchise itself, roughly A$250 million (about K771 million) of the A$600 million (about K1.8 billion) committed by Australia for the project is earmarked for education and social development across PNG and the Pacific.

This investment could expand the work of the League Bilong Laif, a sport-for-development programme established in 2013 in a partnership between the Australian government, the PNG Education Department and the Australian Rugby League Commission.

Preliminary research suggests that programmes like League Bilong Laif can help draw students into the classroom by linking sport with education.

PNG struggles with some of the lowest school retention rates in the region, with only 16 per cent of boys and 12 per cent of girls who enter school at elementary level completing Grade 12.

Strengthening this programme could act as a powerful motivator for students to stay in school and participate in a highly competitive schools-based rugby league program with better preparation to enter the elite national and international competition.

The PNG Chiefs, through the power of broadcast and social media, could reinforce messages about discipline, teamwork and preparation, helping to shape a shared narrative of performance and excellence for young Papua New Guineans.

Governed with integrity and transparency, the pathway from school to elite sport could emphasise the values of fairness and opportunity where selection is based on merit and preparation rather than patronage.

This is a lesson that extends beyond the PNG Chiefs and can be applied to all public institutions.

Whether this potential is realised will depend on design.

If the Chiefs become merely a high-profile entertainment franchise, their nation-building impact will be limited.

But if embedded within a broader strategy of youth development and education pathways, they could model what a genuine “fair go” looks like. – Lowy Institute

Serena Sasingian is a FDC Pacific research fellow at the Lowy Institute, with more than 15 years of experience in development and public policy across government, private, and non-profit sectors. She is the former chief executive officer of Digicel Foundation PNG and co-founder of The Voice Inc., a youth-led development organisation.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Directly voting prime minister:What will it mean for PNG’s political party system?
item.description: IN its final sitting, Parliament will consider the proposal by the Constitutional Law Reform Commission to amend “laws governing the election of prime minister in Papua New Guinea be changed to allow people to vote for prime ministers”. Prime Minister James Marape justified this reform agenda on the grounds that, with “the current legislation, it is hard to fully serve the country”.
item.content:encoded:
IN its final sitting, Parliament will consider the proposal by the Constitutional Law Reform Commission to amend “laws governing the election of prime minister in Papua New Guinea be changed to allow people to vote for prime ministers”.

Prime Minister James Marape justified this reform agenda on the grounds that, with “the current legislation, it is hard to fully serve the country”.

If it becomes law, eligible PNG voters will directly vote for the prime minister of the country. 

In PNG, there are two scenarios for the election of a prime minister.

Elected Members of Parliament (MPs), as provided in s.142 (2) and (3) in the Constitution, during the first sitting of a new term of Parliament nominate and vote the prime minister.

The other scenario happens in a vote of no confidence (s.145 of the Constitution).

In both instances, the people’s representatives as Members of Parliament vote the prime minister.

Making the case for a popular vote of the prime minister

To allow for a popular vote of the prime minister, reforms of this sort must be considered on the basis of whether they will address a pressing deficiency.

What is the problem with how the prime minister is elected presently? 

The prime minister who leads government struggles to focus on the national agendas whilst simultaneously attending to the demands of his Parliamentary coalition partners.

The prime minister in PNG is beholden to the whims of coalition partners, lest these partners break ranks and initiate no confidence votes against him.

Direct voting of the prime minister is therefore seen as the solution where the prime minister is accountable to the voters, and not held to ransom by the demands of his peers on the floor of Parliament.

This reform agenda seeks to insulate the prime minister from the Parliamentary antics of votes of no confidence, and allow the prime minister to concentrate solely on governing.

What to expect in a popular vote for the prime minister?

The unintended effects of this exercise must also be anticipated.

The voting of the prime minister by Parliament is intricately tied to other aspects of PNG’s Westminster system of government.

In s.63 (1) of the Organic Law on the Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates (the Integrity Law), the “registered political party which has endorsed the greatest number of candidates declared elected in the election, and the Head of State, acting with, and in accordance with, the advice of the Electoral Commission, shall invite that registered political party to form the Government”.

In s.63 (4) of the Integrity Law, the political party with the greatest number of candidates in the election is given the first opportunity to nominate its candidate to be elected as prime minister by Parliament.

Since the inception of the Integrity Law, the nominee for prime minister has always been the leader of the political party with the most numbers of candidates declared in a national general election.

This certainty in PNG politics came about through the Integrity Law.

It is in fact the very process James Marape got elected as prime minister (see table).

This provision in the Integrity Law provides incentives for political parties to contest elections, and remain relevant in the political process.

What is the projected outlook for political party development in PNG if the incentives to form government is replaced through a popularly elected prime minister?

The whole question of directly voting the prime minister devolves down to the problem of PNG not having an institutionalised political party system.

The renowned political science scholar, Giovanni Sartori once observed that: “Disciplined parties are a necessary condition for the ‘working’ of parliamentary systems”. 

A useful recommendation was made in late 2020 when a senior constitutional lawyer suggested the idea of criminalising “political camping” as a means of forming executive government.

He further added that this “must included any form of capturing, transporting and detaining any MP for the purpose of amassing the Parliamentary numbers for that purpose (voting the prime minister), including vote of no confidence”.

Not only are offenses in the PNG Criminal Code Act (ss.44, 46, 47 and 48) obvious in such practices, there are undue influences involved (promises of ministerial portfolios and district funding commitments) and it restricts MPs voting on their conscience. 

The Registry of Political Parties is anticipating the implications of this Constitutional amendment, on political parties, as institutions in PNG’s political system.

item.title: No formal training but“mechanic” Cedric can fix vehicles
item.description: THERE is no workshop, no signboard, nor office to indicate to the public that someone is running a small vehicle repair business there on the roadside – to earn a living in bustling Port Moresby. If you drive along Croton Street in Hohola, you will not miss 27-year-old Cedric Erick busy repairing vehicles.
item.content:encoded:
THERE is no workshop, no signboard, nor office to indicate to the public that someone is running a small vehicle repair business there on the roadside – to earn a living in bustling Port Moresby.

If you drive along Croton Street in Hohola, you will not miss 27-year-old Cedric Erick busy repairing vehicles.

“I have no (mechanical engineering) qualification from any technical school. But I have the experience and can do a better job than any learned individual.”

In fact, Cedric, from Lower Mendi in Southern Highlands, was forced to leave school in Grade Nine at Yebi High School after his parents passed away.

“I had dreams like any other village kid. I went to school every day and worked hard. But life was hard during my high school days. There was no one to support me with bus fares, clothes and basic needs.”

In 2017, during the second term while in Grade 9, Cedric dropped out of school.

For two years, he remained in the village, uncertain about his future. Then in 2019, an opportunity arose to travel to Port Moresby.

“They told me there are a lot of opportunities in Port Moresby. So I came without any second thought.”

But like many young men arriving in the capital city, reality was different from expectation.

He moved in with his uncle who is a mechanic. With no qualification and no formal training, Cedric could only assist his uncle with simple tasks.

“I used to just follow him around carrying (vehicle repair) tools.”

There was no garage. People would bring their vehicles and explain what the defect was, and repairs were done.

“We do repairs on the spot and they pay us right away.”

It was informal, it was unpredictable, but it was honest work.

Gradually, Cedric caught on to the basics from watching trained mechanics work. He watched how engines were dismantled. He listened to the sound of faulty engines. He studied the movement of belts, pistons and gears.

“I was able to learn a lot in three years on how to fix a car.”

Then in 2022, he was told to could go solo.

“The mechanic who I call him my half-dad saw that I was becoming really good at what I was doing. He told me: ‘Son, you’re doing better.’”

Those words were golden and meant more than money to Cedric. He was given tools and told to start working on his own. It marked the beginning of a period of self-reliance and independence.

“From then on, I established my own customers. I would work on cars myself (without supervision).”

On busy days, the rewards would be substantial.

“During very busy days, I could repair five to 10 cars. I can walk away with K1,000, K2000 in a day.

“We make money every day. I use it to pay rent, buy household items and my bus fare.”

In the past six years, he has repaired many vehicles – some with engines that would not start, brakes that did not work, or other mechanical problems.

I have no (mechanical engineering) qualification from any technical school. But I have experience and can do a better job than any learned individual.”

There is competition for customers on that roadside along Croton Street. Whoever moves fast secures customers. Some days are dull, with no customers at all. But Cedric does not complain. He has learnt the basic skills in a new trade which is helping him earn an honest living in the busy metropolis.

While he does not have formal training in mechanical engineering, customers have commended him for his outstanding craftsmanship and hard work.

When he arrived in Port Moresby in 2020, he did not have the faintest idea where to start.

“But I have learned that if one works really hard (and is committed to what one does), one will (enjoy a satisfying reward) at the end of the day.”

From nothing at all, to engaging in something he is proud of to earn a living in the city. Cedric’s life journey so far.
